text: The surgeon carefully mobilizes the bowel, which is adherent to the pelvic sidewall, separating it from the ovary visible on the left side of the image. The bowel is on the right side. The nodule is delineated from the pelvic floor muscles at the bottom of the image, extending into the vagina. The ureter and nerves are visible and free from the nodule, which is completely excised.

The surgeon excises a large nodule from the rectum, entering the rectal lumen to ensure complete removal. The excised nodule is sent to pathology. The defect in the rectum is closed using a meticulous three-layer suturing technique, with the second layer currently being performed. An EEA sizer is passed to check for leaks, ensuring the integrity of the bowel anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an appendectomy on a patient with endometriosis of the appendix. Fat and blood supply are separated from the underside of the appendix to its base at the large bowel. Three endo loops are placed to occlude the appendix proximally. The appendix is transected between two loops near the large bowel and one loop on the appendix itself, before being removed and sent to pathology.

The video shows the excision of a stricture from the left ureter due to endometriosis. The stricture is visible in the middle of the image. The surgeon uses scissors to excise the stricture, avoiding thermal damage. Urine is seen exiting the proximal ureter, which remains patent above the stricture. The ureter is then spatulated to enlarge the opening, reducing the risk of future narrowing.

The surgeon places an absorbable suture to re-approximate the two ends of the ureter, avoiding the use of energy devices. A stent is then inserted through the bladder and passed from the lower to the upper edge of the ureter. Additional sutures are placed to close the ureter circumferentially, all tied laparoscopically. The stent remains in place for approximately six weeks. A cystoscopy is performed to ensure the repair is satisfactory.

The surgeon identifies a purple endometriosis lesion on the bladder, visible on cystoscopy. The lesion is located in front of the uterus, away from the ureteral entry points. Adhesions are separated to find a plane between the uterus, cervix, and bladder, allowing the bladder to be mobilized away from the uterus and vagina. The lesion is excised using a harmonic scalpel, penetrating the entire bladder wall, necessitating entry into the bladder to fully remove the lesion under cystoscopic control.

The Foley catheter is visible inside the bladder following the excision of the lesion. The surgeon inspects the ureter entrances on both sides of the bladder to ensure the lesion was not near these sites. The bladder is repaired using laparoscopic suturing in two layers with absorbable sutures to prevent leakage. The second layer of suturing is completed, and the bladder is filled to check for leaks. A cystoscopy is performed to inspect the suture line. Additionally, an endometriosis lesion on the diaphragm is excised, with the lung visible in the background, and closed with sutures.
